commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Come in, an expression which may denote any familiarity, or marriage. (Menochius) Hebrew is in the form of a prohibition, "Come not among (have no connections with) these nations. Neither mention their gods, nor swear (or cause to swear by them. ") The psalmist (Psalm xv. 4,) says, speaking either of idols, (Haydock) or of sinners, Nor will I be mindful of their names by my lips. 16,) says, She shall call me no more Baali, ("my lord "a term applied by wives to their husbands) on account of its reminding one of the idol Baal. Hence David calls Jerobaal, or Gedeon, Jeroboschot, 2 Kings xi. Paul would not have Christians so much as to name the sins of impurity, Ephesians v. The more religious Jews will not even mention an idol, or an unclean animal; and they beg pardon before they speak of a heretic. (Drusius) Some understand that the worship of idols is meant by naming them, as those who invoked the name of Jesus Christ, were his disciples, Acts ix. , 1 Timothy ii. , and Exodus xx. To swear by idols is always sinful, (Exodus xxiii. 13,) while it is an act of religion to swear on proper occasions, by the name of God. Theophrastus (ap. Josephus, contra Apion i. ) observes, that the laws of the Tyrians prohibit the using of foreign oaths, such as that of the Corban, which was peculiar to the Jews.

methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
